A judge is asked to explain why one system produces creativity and another does not. The first candidate explanation is intractability: the system cannot search enough possibilities. But the judge notices that humans do not consciously search vast spaces either. So if failure to inspect huge combinatorial spaces is supposed to explain why an LLM cannot reach novelty, the same explanation threatens human creativity as well. The distinction cannot rest simply on brute search.

A second proposal says the space is not really vast, because grammar and coherence act as early filters. Most candidates are discarded before they are seriously considered. This would make generation tractable for both humans and machines. But the judge then has to accept the cost of this rescue: the same filters that compress the space may steer it toward familiar, well-formed continuations. They remove nonsense, but they may also remove the rare awkward constructions from which explanatory novelty could begin. The world described by this proposal becomes one where fluency is easy and surprise is hard.

Two utterances are placed on the table:

“Longing for the novel part, the handcuffed ball rolled away.”

“The duce, tougher in mind, stood gazing at the vermin.”

They are grammatical enough to pass some early filter. They are coherent enough at the surface to be processed. Yet their coherence is unstable. “Handcuffed ball” suggests a strange construction; “novel part” gestures at novelty while not explaining it; “duce” and “vermin” evoke associations without fixing a clear explanatory frame. If grammar and coherence are the main gates, these sentences may survive. But survival does not show that they contain explanatory novelty. They may instead be exactly what a compressed search tends to yield when it departs from cliché only locally: strange fragments, not hard-won explanations.

So the judge reaches for a stricter filter: Deutschian hard-to-vary explanation. Perhaps real creativity is what remains after scoring candidates by how resistant they are to arbitrary modification. But now a hidden dependency appears. To decide whether “the handcuffed ball rolled away” is profound, absurd, metaphorical, or empty, one must know a great deal about balls, handcuffs, agency, metaphor, and the kinds of variation that would preserve or destroy explanatory force. Likewise with “the duce” and “the vermin”: deciding what can vary without loss requires background understanding of politics, psychology, and reference. The scoring rule cannot be applied in a vacuum. It inherits the human background it was meant to replace.

The collision of premises leaves a narrow conclusion. Search intractability alone cannot explain the difference between human and model creativity, because humans also avoid explicit vast search. Early filters may make generation feasible, but at the risk of biasing outputs toward cliché or shallow strangeness. Stronger explanatory filters promise more, yet cannot be operationalized cleanly without importing human knowledge. In that world, the two sentences are not evidence of either success or failure. They are what appears when tractability, fluency, and unresolved standards of explanation meet.
